speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 12, 1996. Mr. William Dowdell Denson is not a survivor. He was the chief prosecutor for war crimes trials. I am the interviewer, Mark Goldberg. We're in Lawrence, New York. The country is USA, and the language is English. Today is March 12, 1996, Chief Prosecutor William Dowdell Denson. I am the interviewer, Mark Goldberg. We're in Lawrence, New York, in the USA. The language is English, and this is tape number one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Mr. Denson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you be kind enough to say your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: William Dowdell Denson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-E-N-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth? Was it the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other than Bill?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bill. What was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: That makes me 82, going on 83.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What city are we in, and what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lawrence, Nassau County, New York state.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's the United States of America. Please tell me a little bit about your personal history, your family background. Tell me a little bit about your parents, where you grew up, what your childhood was like, high school, college.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born and reared in Birmingham, Alabama. I attended the public schools in Birmingham and graduated in January 1930 from John Herbert Phillips High School in Birmingham. In July, I entered the United States Military Academy at West Point, New York, and became a cadet there, and I graduated from the Military Academy in June of 1934. I resigned my commission as a second lieutenant in the United States Army in July of 1934 and entered Harvard Law School in September of that year. I graduated from Harvard in 1937, in June. I went back to Birmingham, started studying for the bar exam in Alabama. And I took that bar exam and passed it, and was admitted to practice law in October 1937. I, of course, entered into my father's law office, my father being a lawyer. As a matter of fact, my grandfather on both sides of the house-- my maternal and my paternal grandparents were lawyers. Indeed, my grandfather on my mother's side was Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of Alabama, and my grandfather on my father's side was the United States District Attorney for the Northern District of Alabama and was a member of Congress. And speaking of Congress, my great-grandfather on my mother's side was a congressman representing the Middle District of Alabama and was a congressman from 1851 or '52 up until Alabama seceded from the Union at the time of the Civil War. And that grand-- great-grandparent later became the president of what is now known as Auburn. And both sides of the house have been lawyers or judges and positions of that type. As a child, I-- there was nothing abnormally good or bad. I grew up like most weeds do in the South. I had a perfectly normal childhood. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have an older sister Nell and a younger brother, Render. My brother is dead, having passed away in 1992, and my sister is an invalid in a nursing home in Massachusetts. She is three years older than I am. We were a closely-knit family. My brother also graduated from West Point in 1937. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a good time with him when he was a plebe at West Point and I was a first classman, what you would normally know as a senior. And those days were precious to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Growing up in Birmingham in the 1920s and 1930s, did you know any Jewish families? Did you have any Jewish friends or contact with that community at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Our next door neighbor were the Lowens, and they were a very fine Jewish family. There was a family by the name of Josephs that lived across the street from where we were. I had some very dear friends who were Jewish. Ian Friend, Jerome Coppel, just to name two-- come to mind at this particular point. But we all played together, visited in each other's homes, and enjoyed the company of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you, as a young boy growing up, aware at all of Jewish holidays, Jewish customs, through these friendships or through your reading or through any other means?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know the names of the holidays. I do know that there were certain days that they could not come out and play, and that, of course, interfered with our normal routine. But that occurred not too frequently, but frequently enough to be an interruption.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your environment, in your milieu in the South, were you aware of any anti-Semitism? Did you ever see any evidence of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, sir, I never saw any evidence of that at all. As a matter of fact, they would visit in our home, and I would visit in their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I wasn't implying you, personally. I meant, did you ever hear of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was more antipathy toward the Catholics than there was toward the Jews in Birmingham at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me learn a little bit more about your family history. Were you a churchgoing family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, indeed. Not only that, but we were not given spending money. We had to memorize verses from the Bible in order to get our spending money. We had no such thing as an allowance, and we performed chores as members of the family. But any money that we acquired, we acquired by, when we were young, $0.10 a verse, later, quarter a verse, and on up to $1 a verse. As a matter of fact, when I was at West Point, my father said, son, if you memorize and can recite verbatim the 11th chapter of Hebrews, I'll give you $100 in gold. That was in December of 1931. And I learned it. There were 52 verses, and I learned those verses and spoke 'em and recited them to my father, and he gave me 10 $10 gold pieces. And I took that money and gave it back to him [LAUGHS] because I thought he needed it more than I did at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What denomination was that church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Presbyterian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about-- about your teenage years. What did you do that brought you to West Point? Were you an athlete? Were you a good student? What did you do with the other young men in your acquaintance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was president of the student body of John Herbert Phillips High School, where we had approximately 3,000 kids. I was proud of that. I don't know whether you would say I was qualified as a politician or not, but that was a fact. And my brother and I had an automobile, and we rode to school, and we took a load of kids to school every day. And I was slight of build, so there was no way in the world I can play football. I think I weighed about 90 to 100 pounds. We used to have little high school fraternities and had athletic competition there that I participated in, but I never was a star athlete, anything like that. I was president of the Yancy Literary Society in high school and learned Robert's Rules of Order at that time. So other than a normal kid growin' up, there wasn't anything that particularly slated me to be a cadet or join the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide to go to West Point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, that was decided for me. A man by the name of George Huddleston was a congressman from our district, and he was a dear friend of my father's. And sometime in January of 1930, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr. Huddleston had dinner with us at the house. And I graduated from high school in the middle of the year. And I was courtin' a gal at that time, and I had some circles under my eyes. And when I left that dinner table, my father says, George-- speaking to the congressman-- I don't know what I'm gonna do with that boy. He doesn't know how to take care of himself. And George says, send him to West Point. And I threw up my hands, agreeing. Said yeah, send me to West Point. And the next thing I knew, I'd received a notice to appear at the post office for preliminary physical examination, and I attended that and passed it. I had two ears and two eyes and all of my teeth, so that's how I got appointed to West Point. However, there was an inducement then. My father said, son, if you-- I was required to take what was known as not the general entrance exam, but a qualifying examination in algebra, and plane geometry, English grammar, and English and American literature. And Dad said, if you want any coaching, I'll be glad to pay for that. And he said, if you pass that entrance exam, I'll give you $500.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I passed that exam, and I got my 500 bucks and went to New York, and I spent it all before I got to West Point. I had just enough there to pay my railroad fare from Weehawken up to West Point when I entered in July of 1930. Now, during your years at West Point, did you anticipate that you were going to be a career military officer, or had you in mind that you might go to law school someday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't catch on to becoming a lawyer until my senior year at West Point, where we were required to take a course of law. It was general in nature and was directed primarily toward equipping young officers serving on courts martial as trial judge advocates and defense counsel, and, yeah, that's when I first got a feeling for it. And during that time, they had to retire as many old officers as there were graduates in my class to make room for 'em, so they invited anybody that wanted to to actually resign and not have to serve as an officer. And I did that after I found out that they would accept me at Harvard. So I went from Harvard-- from West Point to Harvard in September of 1934. I graduated during the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: During-- during the years that you were at West Point training to be a-- an officer, were there Jewish cadets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, certainly. [? Birkowitz, ?] [? Gilman, ?] two that come to mind in my class.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware at West Point of any anti-anything? Was there anti-Catholicism, anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --any problem at all at West Point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely none. You were your own man in that sense, and we had to go to chapel, and there was a Jewish chapel, and a Catholic chapel, and a Protestant chapel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In those days at West Point, were the-- what were the majority of the students, would you say? What religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Protestant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you made the decision to leave the military, and you went to Harvard Law. Tell me a little bit about that experience and Cambridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was quite a transition because at West Point, their method of instruction was [? really ?] different than it was in law school. At West Point, the cadets did the reciting of the lessons that had been assigned. At Harvard, the professors did the lecturing in law school, and you took notes. And I had one heck of a time trying to take notes in that transition period. But I made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like? What were those three years like? Were they enjoyable? Did you meet all sorts of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very enjoyable. My first year, I lived in a dormitory the first year, and the second and third years, I had an apartment with three of my classmates. There were four of us, and we had an apartment and a cook that cooked our meals for us, just like an extension of home, except we had to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it's deep in the Depression.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was deep in the Depression.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were difficult times in the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like in-- in Cambridge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see a lot of suffering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a lot of suffering. There was a lot of-- I never had to endure it because my grub was always provided for by Uncle Sam, and I was sheltered to that extent, that I was not exposed to the suffering that did prevail during those years of the Depression.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish students in your law class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, quite a number, a lot more there than there were in West Point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any that you became friendly with that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Oh, yeah, sure. There were quite a number of them. And all of 'em were smart as whips, too, very smart.

speaker: interviewer
text: The army, the military was paying your tuition at Harvard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say Uncle Sam provided-- how did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Uncle Sam severed the umbilical cord with me when I resigned my commission in July of 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family was paying for Harvard?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family paid it. My dad paid it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you graduated from Harvard Law School. What did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Birmingham and entered my father's law office--

speaker: interviewer
text: As you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and studied for the bar exam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now, what-- tell me about those years of practice. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had the benefit of my father's experience there, who was a very able trial lawyer, and he started me out trying lawsuits on October the 4th, the day I was admitted to the bar in Alabama. And I had his guidance, and the things that he taught me were invaluable and to the extent that I did a tremendous amount of trial work and appellate work as well. I didn't have to wait around. I was able to go in and-- as soon as I was admitted to the Supreme Court of Alabama, I was eligible to argue cases in the Supreme Court, which I did, the Supreme Court being the court of last resort in Alabama.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of cases were you trying at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: General. My father had a general practice. Ambulance chasing and property.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any criminal work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only had three criminal trials [LAUGHS] before I went to West Point-- when I went back there [INAUDIBLE]. One was for murder. One was burglary in the nighttime, and the third one was armed robbery. And I was able to get an acquittal in the armed robbery case. And in the burglary case, my man had to plead guilty. And he did that because he had a record a mile long. He was an escape artist, and he said, Mr. Denson, don't worry about that. We'll cop a plea, as he put it, and I'll be out before long. And the other case, the murder case, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got him off of-- on involuntary manslaughter for three years, and that was all the criminal work I'd ever done.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now it's December 7th, 1941, Pearl Harbor, and you're a practicing attorney in Alabama. How did you get from there back to West Point soon after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- in November of 1941, I received a letter from Colonel Bukerma who was a professor of economics and government at West Point. He was contacting former graduates who had gone and obtained degrees or other expertise that could be used at West Point, asking if I was willing to come back to West Point and teach. And I, of course, told him I was not unmindful of my obligations, and I would be happy to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was due to his letter of November of 1941 that I went back to West Point, and they took me in in the rank of a captain, which was the rank that my class held at that time. I thought that was mighty fine of 'em. And I taught law there until I left to go overseas in February of 1945. Now, when you were at West Point, was this is an equivalent rank, or were you wearing a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was wearing a uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were back in the military service?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was back into Uncle Sam's arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It must have been a little different teaching law at West Point during the war than it would have been had there not been a war. Can you tell me a little bit about what you were teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the difference basically was in the number of cadets. That was-- there was only 1,200 cadets in the corps of cadets when I graduated, and there were 4,000 when I went back to teach there. And they were spread over the four years. Now, how many that would be for each class is a matter of arithmetic. But it was an expedited course. They had graduation in the winter, and they had a graduation in June. So there were two graduation classes in each year that I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any attention paid to anything related to the war, war crimes eventually or gathering of evidence--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To tell you frankly, I didn't know what a war crime was at that time. I knew-- we just had a smattering of Constitutional law, a little smattering of the conventions-- the Hague and Geneva Conventions. And they were important [INAUDIBLE] because they do relate very much directly toward conduct of individuals, combatants and non-combatants.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So there was a great awareness, obviously, of what was going on in the war, but no particular attention--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: --paid to the legal issues that were going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the years that you were at West Point--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the war was under way, what was your awareness of what was happening to Jews in Europe, your personal awareness?

speaker: interviewee
text: My personal knowledge was absolutely nothing. I didn't have any idea of the privations that the Jewish people were being subjected to.

speaker: interviewer
text: No discussion of this at the Point at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No discussion of that at all at that time, and that was from '41 until the time when I left there in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here, and we'll continue on the next tape.
